description: In the meadow, north of the campground itself. This area had been wet in spring and had lush grass this fall. It was filled with meadowlarks (and for some reason flickers). A goodly number (15-20) of each were in a couple Russian Olives, and I was trying to obtain a good count. The Western Meadowlarks were calling frequently, occasionally singing. I heard a "Bzzzrrt" call about 10 times from the tree; I could not see the bird making the sound, but it sounded like a typical Eastern. I even had time to drag out my phone and play the Eastern call. Excellent match. I then heard the rattle call of an Eastern, which was drier/more mechanical sounding than that of a Western. The bird then flew from the side of the tree, where it had been just out of sight, with a Western. It gave the dry rattle again. The back looked darker than that of the Western, and when fanned, the tail had about 50% more white than the Western's tail did. The Western's tail looked pretty typical for that species. After a false landing or two, they flew about 300 feet away and could not be relocated. They may have crossed a fence onto private property.
